title: Prop X delivery fee cap
prompt: San Francisco is voting on a measure that would cap food delivery app fees (DoorDash, Uber Eats) at 15%. As a resident, would you vote Yes or No? Give your single most important reason in one sentence.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. The 15% cap would help my favorite neighborhood restaurants stay viable without getting squeezed by commission fees that make small orders barely worth their time. I've seen firsthand how these apps charge restaurants so much that small delivery orders become unprofitable, and as someone who values the local spots that make Sunset feel like home, I think protecting those businesses from excessive fees is worth it—even if it means occasionally paying a bit more as a customer or dealing with slightly fewer delivery options.
delivery_app_experiences: A late-night DoorDash order in the Sunset arrived cold after the driver had to make several stacked stops.
A favorite neighborhood restaurant told him app commissions made small delivery orders barely worth accepting.
Uber Eats was useful during a long work sprint, but the final price felt much higher than the menu price.
responses.reason: The 15% cap would help my favorite neighborhood restaurants stay viable without getting squeezed by commission fees that make small orders barely worth their time.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes. The Korean restaurant I like asked customers to order directly because app commissions were hurting their margins, and a 15% cap would protect local businesses without killing the delivery option entirely. I've seen firsthand how these platforms squeeze restaurants—and honestly, between the cancelled Excelsior order and the fees that double the cost of a simple meal, the current system isn't working for anyone except the apps themselves.
delivery_app_experiences: A delivery app cancelled his Excelsior dinner order after forty minutes because no driver accepted the trip.
He noticed service fees and small-order fees often made a simple meal cost almost twice as much.
A Korean restaurant he likes asked customers to order directly because app commissions were hurting margins.
responses.reason: The Korean restaurant I like asked customers to order directly because app commissions were hurting their margins, and a 15% cap would protect local businesses without killing the delivery option entirely.

responses.response: Yes. The restaurant note asking regulars to call directly showed me that platform fees are crushing local businesses that make Mission feel like home, even though I depend on delivery apps during heavy work weeks. I've seen the convenience firsthand when systems work is intense, but I've also paid extra for priority delivery that didn't deliver on time—so the platforms aren't always worth their cut. A 15% cap protects the neighborhood spots I want to keep around without killing the service entirely.
delivery_app_experiences: During a demanding work week, delivery apps helped him avoid losing time to meal pickup.
A Mission restaurant included a note asking regulars to call directly because platform fees were too high.
He once paid extra for priority delivery and still received food later than the estimate.
responses.reason: The restaurant note asking regulars to call directly showed me that platform fees are crushing local businesses that make Mission feel like home, even though I depend on delivery apps during heavy work weeks.

responses.response: I'm voting No. I worry that a 15% cap would push delivery apps to raise consumer prices or cut service quality, making the convenience I rely on during crunch time at work either more expensive or unavailable. I've relied on Uber Eats when I'm deep in a release cycle and can't step away—I need that option to stay viable, even if the current system has frustrations like misleading promos and impersonal refunds. A cap sounds good on paper, but if it means fewer restaurants available or higher fees passed on to me as a renter already watching every line item in this city, I'd rather deal with the transparency problem directly than risk losing access altogether.
delivery_app_experiences: She relied on Uber Eats during a software release week and valued the convenience despite the cost.
A Nob Hill order arrived missing items, and the app refund process felt automated and impersonal.
She has seen delivery promotions disappear after fees and taxes were added at checkout.
responses.reason: I worry that a 15% cap would push delivery apps to raise consumer prices or cut service quality, making the convenience I rely on during crunch time at work either more expensive or unavailable.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. The markup I saw when ordering team lunch on DoorDash was excessive, and I've heard directly from local cafe owners in Haight that these commissions are crushing their margins. As someone managing budgets in my facilities work, I know how thin operating margins can be, and these apps are taking too much from neighborhood businesses that are already struggling with San Francisco's costs. The delayed delivery that forced me to reorder directly showed me restaurants can do better without the middleman taking such a big cut.
delivery_app_experiences: A Haight cafe owner told him delivery app commissions cut deeply into already thin operating margins.
He used DoorDash for a team lunch and was surprised by how much the restaurant prices were marked up.
A delayed delivery forced him to reorder food directly from a nearby restaurant before an evening shift.
responses.reason: The markup I saw when ordering team lunch on DoorDash was excessive, and I've heard directly from local cafe owners in Haight that these commissions are crushing their margins.

responses.response: Yes. I've watched too many restaurants in Chinatown get squeezed by platform commissions that eat their margins while the apps change terms whenever they want, and a 15% cap would give small operators the stability they need to survive. As a food service manager, I see firsthand how delivery orders bring customers but don't bring much profit when platforms take 25-30% off the top. The unpredictability is what really kills you—menu prices go up, service suffers, and we're still barely breaking even. A cap would at least give restaurants some breathing room and make the numbers work better for neighborhood businesses trying to stay afloat in this expensive city.
delivery_app_experiences: As a food service manager, he has watched restaurants raise app menu prices to cover platform commissions.
He has seen delivery orders disrupt kitchen timing during busy service without bringing much profit.
He appreciates that apps bring incremental customers, but he worries the platform terms change without warning.
responses.reason: I've watched too many restaurants in Chinatown get squeezed by platform commissions that eat their margins while the apps change terms whenever they want, and a 15% cap would give small operators the stability they need to survive.

responses.response: Yes. I've watched delivery fees turn an affordable meal into something I can't justify on my income, and a 15% cap would make it possible to actually use these apps without splitting orders or skipping meals. When you're living on what I make in retail and paying rent in this city, every extra fee hits hard—I've literally had to team up with coworkers just to make delivery affordable, and I've seen Richmond spots offer pickup discounts because the app fees were killing them. A cap protects both people like me trying to grab food after a long shift and the local restaurants that are already squeezed.
delivery_app_experiences: He ordered delivery after a retail shift and felt frustrated when fees made a cheap meal unaffordable.
A Richmond restaurant he likes offered a discount for pickup because app fees were too steep.
He once split an app order with coworkers because delivery was only affordable when shared.
responses.reason: I've watched delivery fees turn an affordable meal into something I can't justify on my income, and a 15% cap would make it possible to actually use these apps without splitting orders or skipping meals.

responses.response: I'm voting No on the delivery app fee cap. As someone who depends on these platforms when I'm stuck at the office late, I've already seen restaurants charging more on apps than for direct orders—and a 15% fee cap will just incentivize them to hike those menu prices further to make up the difference, squeezing my household budget even more without actually fixing the cost problem.
delivery_app_experiences: He uses delivery apps occasionally when work runs late and values predictable access to many restaurants.
A Portola order took much longer than estimated because the driver came from across town.
He has compared app prices with direct ordering and noticed restaurants often charge more on platforms.
responses.reason: The cap will likely push restaurants to raise menu prices on apps even higher than they already do, eroding the predictable service access I rely on when work runs late.

responses.response: Yes. I've watched too many Mission businesses struggle with commission fees that cut into their survival margins while I can barely afford delivery myself. Living on $10,400 a year, I've abandoned orders when the fees stack up, and the taqueria around the corner explicitly asked us to stop using apps because the commissions were killing them—so capping those fees at 15% protects the local places that keep this neighborhood alive, even if I rarely use delivery anyway.
delivery_app_experiences: On a tight budget, he abandoned several delivery orders after fees made the total too high.
A Mission taqueria he visits asked customers to avoid apps because commissions were hard on the business.
He appreciated delivery during a rainy week but felt guilty that the driver seemed rushed and underpaid.
responses.reason: I've watched too many Mission businesses struggle with commission fees that cut into their survival margins while I can barely afford delivery myself.

responses.response: Yes. I've seen how delivery app fees hurt both the North Beach restaurants trying to stay afloat and the families I work with who need affordable access to food when work schedules are impossible. When I used an app while sick, the fees felt like a luxury I could barely justify on my income, and the local restaurants here have been clear about how those platform cuts threaten their survival. A 15% cap protects both the small businesses that make this neighborhood work and the working families who shouldn't have to choose between convenience and eating through their budget.
delivery_app_experiences: A client family mentioned relying on delivery apps when work schedules made grocery shopping difficult.
He has seen North Beach restaurants encourage direct ordering to avoid platform fees.
He once used an app while sick and valued the access, but the fees made it feel like a luxury.
responses.reason: I've seen how delivery app fees hurt both the North Beach restaurants trying to stay afloat and the families I work with who need affordable access to food when work schedules are impossible.

responses.response: Yes. I've abandoned orders mid-checkout when fees made a simple meal unaffordable on my $40,000 income, and capping those fees would make delivery accessible without forcing me to skip meals or stretch my already tight budget. Living in Sunset on a paralegal salary, every dollar counts—and I've seen firsthand how these apps price out working people while local restaurants beg us to order direct. A 15% cap feels like basic protection for renters like me who are already stretched thin by San Francisco's cost of living.
delivery_app_experiences: He stopped using a delivery app mid-checkout after the fees pushed a modest meal beyond his budget.
A Sunset restaurant included a flyer asking customers to order through its own website instead.
He once received someone else's order and spent too much time navigating app support for a refund.
responses.reason: I've abandoned orders mid-checkout when fees made a simple meal unaffordable on my $40,000 income, and capping those fees would make delivery accessible without forcing me to skip meals or stretch my already tight budget.

responses.response: I would vote Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. I've seen how those app fees squeeze both the families in Bayview who need delivery help and the restaurant owners trying to keep prices fair for neighbors, so a cap would protect the community I care about. As a teacher, I see parents working multiple jobs and I know what happens when basic services become unaffordable—it's the families already stretched thin who suffer most, and our local businesses deserve a fair chance to serve the neighborhood without being bled dry by these tech companies.
delivery_app_experiences: A Bayview family she knows used delivery while caring for a sick child, but the cost was a strain.
A local restaurant owner told her app fees made it harder to keep prices reasonable for neighbors.
She has ordered delivery after school events and noticed the driver seemed pressured by tight timing.
responses.reason: I've seen how those app fees squeeze both the families in Bayview who need delivery help and the restaurant owners trying to keep prices fair for neighbors, so a cap would protect the community I care about.

responses.response: Yes. I've seen too many Chinatown restaurants I care about lose money to app fees while struggling to stay afloat in this expensive city. I've watched restaurant owners here ask regulars to call directly because the delivery platforms take such a big cut, and these are businesses that anchor our neighborhood. The apps provide convenience, sure—I've used them myself on rainy nights—but not at the cost of bleeding out the places that make Chinatown what it is. A 15% cap seems like a reasonable compromise that lets the platforms operate while giving local restaurants a fighting chance to survive San Francisco's brutal economics.
delivery_app_experiences: He has watched Chinatown restaurants ask regulars to call directly because delivery app fees erode revenue.
A delivery order was convenient during a rainy evening, but he felt the neighborhood restaurant got the worse deal.
He once received a cold meal after a driver handled multiple app orders at once.
responses.reason: I've seen too many Chinatown restaurants I care about lose money to app fees while struggling to stay afloat in this expensive city.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. The cap would help the Bayview restaurants I care about keep more of their money instead of losing it to commission fees that hurt local businesses and make food unaffordable for people on tight budgets like mine. I've seen the sign at my neighborhood spot asking for direct orders to avoid those fees, and I know from my own experience how the charges stack up when you're living on $25,000 a year. This measure protects both the restaurants trying to survive and residents who can't afford to pay extra just to get food delivered.

When he did order during bad weather, the delivery charge and tip made him reconsider using the app again.
responses.reason: The cap would help the Bayview restaurants I care about keep more of their money instead of losing it to commission fees that hurt local businesses and make food unaffordable for people on tight budgets like mine.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes. Mission restaurants I depend on have told me these delivery fees are expensive for their business, and protecting local businesses matters more to me than slightly slower app growth. I use the apps when I'm stuck at work, and I've seen the markup—but I also know the neighborhood places are under enough pressure already without getting squeezed by tech platforms taking huge cuts. A 15% cap feels like a fair middle ground that keeps delivery available while giving our local spots a better chance to survive in this expensive city.

responses.reason: Mission restaurants I depend on have told me these delivery fees are expensive for their business, and protecting local businesses matters more to me than slightly slower app growth.

responses.response: Yes. I've watched those app commissions drain money from the restaurants where I work, making delivery orders less profitable than walk-ins while drivers still get low payouts. The fees squeeze everyone except the app companies—my workplace, the drivers who wait in our lobby, and customers like me who've paid too much after a late shift. A 15% cap might actually balance things out for the people doing the actual work.

I heard firsthand from a restaurant owner that the commission structure was painful, and protecting local Excelsior businesses matters more than preserving unlimited platform profits. responses.response: I'd vote Yes. The cap would make delivery more affordable for people like me who struggle with the fees on a tight budget, and it might help local restaurants compete without those apps taking such a big cut. I've seen how those fees pile up—even with a promotion, taxes, fees, and tip made one order feel way too costly. And that Sunset restaurant I like literally charges less for direct pickup because the app takes so much. On my income, every dollar counts, and this feels like it would help both customers and the small businesses trying to survive in this expensive city.

responses.response: No. I've seen how small restaurants struggle with margins already, and a cap might just push apps to cut service coverage or hours in neighborhoods like Portola where delivery logistics are already tough. I use these apps when client meetings run late, and I know Portola restaurant owners complain about commissions—but if the platforms pull back service or raise other costs, everyone here loses access. The risk isn't worth mandating a number that might sound fair but breaks the business model for reaching us.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. My dispatch work has shown me firsthand how these platforms squeeze both drivers and local restaurants with fees that destabilize the whole chain, and a 15% cap would protect the North Beach spots I care about from getting bled dry. I've watched that restaurant I like push direct ordering just to survive the commission costs, and I know from coordinating logistics how the pressure trickles down to drivers and timing. The convenience is real, but so is the extraction—and in a neighborhood where small businesses are already hanging by a thread, we need to draw a line somewhere.
